title: Stark-tunable O-band single-photon sources based on deterministically fabricated quantum dot--circular Bragg gratings on silicon
authors: Sarthak Tripathi, Kartik Gaur, Priyabrata Mudi, Peter Ludewig, Alexander Kosarev, Kerstin Volz, Imad Limame, Stephan Reitzenstein
abstract: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) offer outstanding quantum-optical properties, making them highly attractive for quantum information technologies. However, combining wide-range electrical tunability, efficient photon extraction, elevated-temperature operation, monolithic silicon integration, and telecom-wavelength compatibility remains a major challenge. Here, we demonstrate electrically contacted circular Bragg grating (eCBG) resonators incorporating InGaAs QDs directly grown on silicon, enabling bright single-photon emission in the telecom O-band. Deterministic electron-beam lithography and a ridge-based vertical p--i--n diode architecture enable precise device integration and electrical control of individual emitters. The QD--eCBGs exhibit a quantum-confined Stark shift of approximately 16 nm (11 meV) at 4 K, representing a record for QDs embedded in nanophotonic structures at telecom wavelengths. This is achieved alongside a photon extraction efficiency of $(21.7 \pm 3.0)\%$ into the first lens, while maintaining excellent radiative properties and high single-photon purity, with $g^{(2)}(0)=0.0078 \pm 0.0012$ below saturation and $g^{(2)}(0)=0.0183 \pm 0.0021$ at saturation under pulsed excitation. Robust antibunching persists up to 77 K, with $g^{(2)}(0)=0.0663 \pm 0.0056$, enabling operation with liquid-nitrogen or compact Stirling cryocoolers. Furthermore, spatially separated QD--eCBGs can be electrically tuned into spectral resonance without degrading photon statistics. These results establish a silicon-compatible, electrically addressable telecom O-band quantum light platform combining wide spectral tunability, high single-photon purity, and elevated-temperature operation, providing a scalable route toward practical photonic quantum networks.
summary: This paper presents a breakthrough in quantum photonics by integrating InGaAs quantum dots directly onto silicon for telecom-band operation. The researchers developed an electrically contacted circular Bragg grating that allows for a record-breaking 16 nm wavelength tuning via the Stark effect. The device maintains high single-photon purity and efficient photon extraction even at temperatures up to 77 K. This platform offers a scalable route for building large-scale, silicon-compatible quantum communication networks.
main_problem: The challenge of creating single-photon sources that simultaneously offer high extraction efficiency, wide electrical tunability, and compatibility with silicon-based telecom-wavelength quantum networks.
main_result: Demonstrated a silicon-compatible, electrically tunable InGaAs quantum dot platform in the telecom O-band that achieves a record 16 nm Stark tuning range and high single-photon purity up to 77 K.
method: The researchers used MOCVD growth of III-V layers on silicon, deterministic electron-beam lithography for device fabrication, and micro-photoluminescence spectroscopy combined with Hanbury Brown and Twint measurements.
model_or_system: The platform consists of InGaAs quantum dots embedded in electrically contacted circular Bragg grating (eCBG) resonators, integrated on a silicon substrate via a GaP nucleation and defect-filtering layer architecture.
key_observables: Photon extraction efficiency (PEE), second-order autocorrelation g2(0), spectral tuning range (nm/meV), radiative lifetime, and single-photon purity.
important_parameters: Telecom O-band (1302 nm), temperature range 4 K to 77 K, Stark shift of 16 nm, and photon extraction efficiency of 21.7%.
main_assumptions: The design assumes that the loss introduced by the electrical ridge-based architecture can be minimized to maintain high optical performance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the heterostructure schematic and 3D device architecture; Figure 5 displays temperature-dependent photoluminescence and photon statistics; Figure 6 demonstrates the electrical tuning of two separate emitters into spectral resonance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the integration challenge, describes the MOCVD growth and device fabrication, presents the optical and electrical characterization of the eCBG resonators, demonstrates the large Stark tuning range, and concludes with the demonstration of spectral alignment between two separate emitters.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a scalable, silicon-integrated method for controlling the spectral properties of single-photon emitters, which is essential for interference-based quantum protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Layer and device design of the QD-eCBG quantum light sources. (a) Schematic of the monolithically grown III-V heterostructure on silicon (b) Three-dimensional illustration of the electrically contacted circular Bragg grating, showing the central mesa, concentric Bragg rings, and the electrical ridge required for biasing.

Fig. 2 Deterministic integration of InGaAs QDs into eCBGs with enhanced O-band emission. (a) CL map overlaid on a secondary-electron image, identifying spatially localized InGaAs QDs relative to alignment markers. (b) CL map after marker-based lithography, confirming successful integration of the selected QD into the eCBG. (c) Tilted SEM image of a fabricated device, showing well-defined sidewalls and the electrical access ridge. (d) CL spectra at 20 K before and after integra- tion, demonstrating enhanced emission near 1300 nm due to cavity coupling and improved vertical photon extraction.

Fig. 3 Excitonic emission and electrically controlled wavelength tuning of a single QD in an eCBG device. (a) µPL spectrum of eCBG1 recorded at 4 K under pulsed picosecond excitation at 1225 nm, showing emission from the neutral exciton (X, black), charged exciton (X±, green), biexciton (XX, red), and charged biexciton (XX±, yellow). Line assignments are based on excitation- power and polarization-dependent µPL measurements. (b) False-color map of bias-dependent µPL spectra acquired at 4 K, demonstrating electrically controlled wavelength tuning of an excitonic transition via the QCSE.

Fig. 4 Time-resolved µPL and second-order correlation confirming high-purity single- photon emission from a QD-eCBG. (a) Time-resolved µPL measurement showing a single- exponential decay with an extracted spontaneous emission decay time of 0.775 ± 0.022 ns. (b) Second-order autocorrelation measurement under pulsed excitation yielding fitted g(2)(0) = 0.0078 ± 0.0012, corresponding to a single-photon purity of 99.22 ± 0.18%, comparable to state-of-the-art pla- nar InGaAs QD single-photon sources.

Fig. 5 Temperature-dependent µPL and photon autocorrelation of a QD-eCBG device. (a) Waterfall plot of µPL spectra recorded from an eCBG1 device at temperatures of 4, 20, 40, and 77 K under pulsed excitation, showing well-resolved QDs emission up to liquid-nitrogen temperature. The X transition is indicated by the gray areas. (b) Second-order photon autocorrelation measure- ments g(2)(τ) of the X transition acquired at the same temperatures and at saturation pump power, demonstrating preserved single-photon emission characteristics up to 77 K.

Fig. 6 Electrical tuning of two QD-eCBGs into spectral resonance with preserved single- photon emission. (a) Bias-dependent tuning of two excitonic emission lines from two QD-eCBG devices, demonstrating that they can be brought into mutual resonance by electrical control. (b) µPL spectra recorded at the resonance bias, confirming identical emission energies. (c) Time-resolved µPL measurements at the resonance bias, revealing comparable radiative lifetimes of both devices. (d) Second-order autocorrelation measurements g(2)(τ) acquired at the resonance bias, showing preserved single-photon purity for both emitters.
